title: King & Queen vs King
summary: The most important mate to learn

steps.title: Classic Mate
steps.text:
## K + Q vs K — the staple checkmate

This is the **first checkmate every player must own**. With king and queen against a lone king, the side with the queen always wins from any starting position — usually in **under ten moves**.

### What this endgame looks like

- One side has **king + queen**.
- The other has only the **king**.
- No pawns, no minor pieces, no rooks — pure technique.

### The key concept: the box and the wall

The queen builds an invisible **wall** that the lone king cannot cross, while your own king walks up to **support** the final blow. The only thing you must avoid is **stalemate** — that is the single hidden danger of this ending.

### Procedure to convert

1. Use the queen to **shrink the king's box**, staying a knight's-move away when possible.
2. **March your king up** to a square next to where you want to deliver mate.
3. Drive the enemy king to the **edge** of the board.
4. Deliver mate with the **queen on the edge**, defended (directly or remotely) by your king.

### Common mistakes

- Checking with the queen on every move — the king dances around forever.
- Trapping the lone king with **no legal squares** while the queen does *not* give check — this is **stalemate** and a draw.
- Forgetting to bring your own king up; the queen alone cannot mate.

### A model finish

From the position shown — Black king on **e8**, White king on **e6**, queen on **e5** — White plays {{Qb8#}} or {{Qh8#}}: the queen seals the 8th rank and the king on e6 covers d7, e7, and f7.

> The queen does the cutting, the king does the catching.

steps.prompt: Deliver **checkmate in one**. The enemy king is on the edge and your king is already supporting — finish the job.
steps.hint: Slide the queen along the **8th rank** so every escape square is covered or attacked.
